title: Fancy Closet Tower
summary: A tall plywood shelf tower for a closet system — adjustable shelves, a face frame, and baseboard trim so it reads built-in, not boxy.
description:
A closet tower is the backbone of a built-in closet: a tall plywood cabinet of shelves that anchors a wall of hanging rods and cubbies. This one is a straightforward 3/4" plywood box, but three finish details are what make it look like millwork instead of a shop shelf.

First, adjustable shelves on pins — flexible, but they come with a catch: you have to trim each shelf down (about 1/4" in width and depth here) to clear the edge banding and the pin spacing, or they won't drop in. Second, edge banding on the exposed plywood edges, ironed on and trimmed flush. Third, a baseboard base that matches the closet's existing trim, mitered around the bottom so the tower grows out of the wall rather than sitting on it.

One structural note that's easy to skip: if your shelves are all adjustable, you need the 1/4" plywood back. With no fixed shelves tying the sides together, the box will splay in the middle under load — the back is what holds it square.

tools.note: Or 2" self-tapping screws. The carcass joins with 3/4" pocket holes.
Iron-on banding hides the plywood edges.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood
materials.note: Cut into three 15-3/4" wide x 8-ft strips. More if you want additional shelving beyond the tower.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood
materials.note: Back — needed if shelves are adjustable, to keep the tower from splaying.

cutList.note: Trim adjustable shelves down ~1/4" in width and depth for banding and pin clearance.

steps.title: Build the carcass box
steps.body: Build the basic box with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket screws, placing the pockets on the outside top and bottom where they'll be hidden. Keep it square.

steps.title: Drill shelf-pin holes and band the shelves
steps.body: Use a shelf-pin jig to drill the adjustable-shelf holes. Iron edge banding onto the exposed plywood edges of the shelves and sides. Then trim each adjustable shelf down (~1/4" overall here) so it clears the pins and banding — measure your own pins, they vary.

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: If you're running all adjustable shelves, add the 1/4" plywood back — without fixed shelves tying the sides, the tower will bow out in the middle. Attach with glue and 3/4" brad nails.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Build the face frame first with pocket holes, then attach the whole assembly to the front of the tower with glue and finish nails. It dresses the front and hides the plywood edges.

steps.title: Add the base and header trim
steps.body: Trim out the base in baseboard matching the closet's existing trim, mitering the corners and attaching to the 2x2 base with a finish nailer and glue. Add the optional 3/4" x 3/4" corner moulding to the top header. Then fill, sand, and finish.

images.alt: A tall closet storage tower with adjustable plywood shelves and a face frame
